extra.reasoning:
vision_loss appears in exactly one rule: interaction_drop (priority 100), which fires when hemoptysis AND abdominal_pain AND sweating AND chest_pain AND vomiting AND vision_loss are all true, setting acuity := self_care (the lowest acuity). Because priority 100 dominates every other rule, this rule controls the outcome whenever it matches.

Take the baseline input with the five non-vision conditions true and vision_loss FALSE (hemoptysis, abdominal_pain, sweating, chest_pain, vomiting = true; vision_loss = false; others false). The interaction_drop rule does not fire (vision_loss is false). The matching rules are raise_chest_pain (pri 3 -> emergent), raise_abdominal_pain (pri 3 -> emergent), raise_vomiting (pri 2 -> urgent), raise_sweating (pri 2 -> urgent), raise_hemoptysis (pri 2 -> urgent). Highest priority wins -> acuity = emergent.

Now flip vision_loss to TRUE, everything else unchanged. All six conditions of interaction_drop are now satisfied, so it fires at priority 100 -> acuity := self_care, overriding all the priority-3 emergent rules. Result acuity = self_care.

In the enum order [self_care, routine, urgent, emergent], self_care < emergent. Setting vision_loss true LOWERED acuity from emergent to self_care, directly violating the monotonicity property (vision_loss true must never lower acuity). The reported witness is the input with vision_loss = true; its self_care result is lower than the emergent result of its vision_loss = false counterpart.
